content.text: Mapping Python objects directly to relational database tables eliminates most hand-written SQL from application code. An Object-Relational Mapper (ORM) translates method calls on model instances —

— into parameterized SQL, manages connection pooling, tracks dirty state, and loads relationships on demand. For agentic-AI backends built with FastAPI, ORMs like SQLAlchemy 2.0 and SQLModel are the standard way to persist sessions, runs, tool events, and audit logs.

An ORM is a library that bridges the structural mismatch between object-oriented code and relational databases. You declare Python classes whose attributes correspond to table columns; the ORM generates the SQL DDL for schema creation, the DML for inserts and updates, and the SELECT statements for queries — all from the class definitions and method calls. The core pattern is that each Python class maps to one table and each instance maps to one row.
SQLAlchemy is the dominant Python ORM. Its 2.0 API (released in 2023) introduced fully typed
query style for both sync and async usage.
integrates with Python's asyncio event loop via

(SQLite), making it a natural fit for FastAPI dependency injection. SQLModel (by FastAPI's author) layers Pydantic v2 validation on top of SQLAlchemy, giving a single class that serves both as API schema and ORM model.

from sqlalchemy.orm import DeclarativeBase, Mapped, mapped_column, relationship
from sqlalchemy import String, ForeignKey, DateTime, func
from uuid import UUID, uuid4
from datetime import datetime

class Base(DeclarativeBase):
    pass

class AgentSession(Base):
    __tablename__ = "agent_sessions"

    id: Mapped[UUID] = mapped_column(primary_key=True, default=uuid4)
    user_id: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(String(64), index=True)
    created_at: Mapped[datetime] = mapped_column(server_default=func.now())
    runs: Mapped[list["Run"]] = relationship(back_populates="session",
                                              cascade="all, delete-orphan")

class Run(Base):
    __tablename__ = "runs"

    id: Mapped[UUID] = mapped_column(primary_key=True, default=uuid4)
    session_id: Mapped[UUID] = mapped_column(ForeignKey("agent_sessions.id"))
    status: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(String(32), default="pending")
    started_at: Mapped[datetime] = mapped_column(server_default=func.now())
    session: Mapped[AgentSession] = relationship(back_populates="runs")
    tool_events: Mapped[list["ToolEvent"]] = relationship(back_populates="run",
                                                           cascade="all, delete-orphan")

class ToolEvent(Base):
    __tablename__ = "tool_events"

    id: Mapped[int] = mapped_column(primary_key=True, autoincrement=True)
    run_id: Mapped[UUID] = mapped_column(ForeignKey("runs.id"), index=True)
    tool_name: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(String(64))
    input_tokens: Mapped[int] = mapped_column(default=0)
    output_tokens: Mapped[int] = mapped_column(default=0)
    run: Mapped[Run] = relationship(back_populates="tool_events")

Agentic-AI services generate high volumes of structured data: every invocation creates a run record, each tool call appends an event, and every checkpoint updates state. Writing raw SQL for this workload produces hundreds of repetitive INSERT/UPDATE/SELECT strings scattered across the codebase. When the schema evolves — adding a cost column to ToolEvent, or adding an index — every query string becomes a liability. ORMs centralize schema logic in one place: the model class.

Security is an equally critical driver. Any codebase that concatenates user input into SQL strings is vulnerable to SQL injection. ORMs emit parameterized queries by default — user-supplied values are always passed as bind parameters, never interpolated into the SQL text. Developers rarely need to think about escaping because the ORM never gives them an unsafe path.

Finally, ORMs align database persistence with the rest of the Python stack. A FastAPI route models its request body with Pydantic; having an ORM model with the same field names and types in the persistence layer makes data flow transparent. SQLModel makes this alignment literal — the Pydantic model
— CRUD operations and relationship traversal in Python; no repeated SQL strings.
— eliminates SQL injection for all ORM-generated queries without developer effort.
— model classes become the authoritative schema definition; Alembic generates migration scripts automatically.
in Python; the ORM emits the JOIN or secondary SELECT.
integrates natively with FastAPI and asyncio, enabling non-blocking database I/O.
— swap PostgreSQL for SQLite in tests; the ORM translates the same query objects to each dialect's SQL.
give static type checkers full visibility into model attributes.
— iterating over a list and accessing a relationship per item issues one extra SELECT per row. Left unmanaged, a loop over 200 runs fetching tool_events each time sends 201 queries.
— window functions, LATERAL joins, recursive CTEs, or heavy aggregations are awkward to express in ORM query syntax. Dropping to
or a raw SQL string is sometimes the cleaner choice.
— accessing a lazy-loaded relationship from an async context raises
because there is no coroutine to await the implicit I/O. Async code must eagerly load all relationships at query time.
— the session lifecycle (pending, persistent, detached, expired states), the identity map, the unit-of-work flush, and the difference between
— when a query runs slowly, the developer must enable SQL logging to see what the ORM actually sent to the database, then reason backwards from the generated SQL to the Python code that produced it.
— for very high-throughput bulk inserts (millions of rows),
or raw COPY FROM are orders of magnitude faster than calling
Session and the unit of work
). It is a short-lived object representing one database transaction and one unit of work. When you call
, the object moves from the transient state to the pending state — it exists in Python but not yet in the database. When you call

(or the sync equivalent), the session flushes all pending changes: it computes which objects are new (INSERT), which have changed attributes (UPDATE), and which have been deleted (DELETE), then sends those statements in dependency order so foreign key constraints are respected.

Within one session, each database row has exactly one Python object. If you query for a Run with id=42 twice, the session returns the same Python object both times — it does not issue two SELECT statements. This guarantee (the identity map) prevents update-lost-update races within a single request and makes the object graph consistent. Once the session is closed, all objects become detached and any attribute access that requires a database round-trip raises a
expressions as the query API. A

call builds an abstract syntax tree (AST). When the session executes it, the engine's dialect compiler walks the AST and produces the SQL string appropriate for the target database (e.g., PostgreSQL uses
). The same Python query produces correct SQL for any supported backend.
Relationships are not loaded by default when you fetch a model; this is called lazy loading. In a synchronous context, accessing
implicitly issues a SELECT — this is convenient but produces N+1 queries in loops. SQLAlchemy offers two eager-loading strategies for relationships:
— after the primary SELECT, issues a second SELECT with an IN clause to load all related rows at once. Best for one-to-many relationships with moderate numbers of rows.
— adds a LEFT OUTER JOIN to the primary query. Best for many-to-one relationships where you want one round-trip for both the parent and child.

The diagram below shows the three layers of a typical SQLAlchemy-based agentic backend. The application layer owns the model classes and calls the session API. The ORM core layer handles the unit of work, identity map, query compilation, and relationship loading. The database layer provides connection pooling, dialect-specific SQL, and schema migration.

attrs.alt: SQLAlchemy ORM architecture: application, ORM core, and database layers

content.text: A request arrives at a FastAPI route. The route depends on an injected

from a context manager. The session collects all model mutations during the request, then on commit the unit of work translates them to ordered SQL statements — INSERTs before UPDATEs, parents before children. The connection pool reuses database connections across requests. Alembic manages the DDL side: it compares the current ORM model definitions against the live schema and generates migration scripts.

Use an ORM for the standard persistence operations in an agentic service: creating and updating session, run, and tool-event records; querying a user's recent runs; loading a run with its events for display. These are well-structured, entity-centric operations that map naturally to ORM patterns.
Analytics queries with window functions, GROUPING SETS, or complex aggregations that would require more code to express through the ORM than as plain SQL.
or PostgreSQL COPY is needed for throughput.
Database-specific features not abstracted by SQLAlchemy (e.g., PostgreSQL full-text search with ts_vector, advisory locks, NOTIFY/LISTEN).
Reporting queries joining many tables with custom column expressions that the ORM query builder makes unwieldy.
The two approaches can coexist. SQLAlchemy's
function lets you embed a raw SQL string inside an otherwise ORM-managed session, so the connection and transaction remain under ORM control even when the query syntax is hand-written.
Create the engine once at application startup with connection URL and pool settings. Use
during development to log all SQL.

# database.py
from sqlalchemy.ext.asyncio import create_async_engine, AsyncSession, async_sessionmaker

DATABASE_URL = "postgresql+asyncpg://user:pass@localhost/agentdb"

engine = create_async_engine(
    DATABASE_URL,
    pool_size=10,
    max_overflow=20,
    echo=False,          # set True in development to log SQL
)

AsyncSessionLocal = async_sessionmaker(
    engine,
    class_=AsyncSession,
    expire_on_commit=False,   # keep attrs accessible after commit
)

async def get_db() -> AsyncSession:
    """FastAPI dependency — yields one session per request."""
    async with AsyncSessionLocal() as session:
        yield session
to create tables. For production, use Alembic: it auto-generates migration scripts by comparing model definitions to the live schema.

# One-time setup
pip install alembic
alembic init alembic

# Edit alembic/env.py: import your Base and point to your engine URL

# Generate a migration after changing a model
alembic revision --autogenerate -m "add cost_usd to tool_events"

# Apply migrations
alembic upgrade head

# Roll back last migration
alembic downgrade -1
Wrap session calls in a thin repository class. This keeps business logic out of route handlers and makes the data layer testable — inject a mock session in unit tests.

# repositories/run_repo.py
from sqlalchemy.ext.asyncio import AsyncSession
from sqlalchemy import select
from sqlalchemy.orm import selectinload
from uuid import UUID
from models import Run, ToolEvent

class RunRepository:
    def __init__(self, session: AsyncSession):
        self.session = session

    async def create(self, session_id: UUID, status: str = "pending") -> Run:
        run = Run(session_id=session_id, status=status)
        self.session.add(run)
        await self.session.flush()   # assigns run.id without committing
        return run

    async def get_with_events(self, run_id: UUID) -> Run | None:
        stmt = (
            select(Run)
            .where(Run.id == run_id)
            .options(selectinload(Run.tool_events))
        )
        result = await self.session.execute(stmt)
        return result.scalar_one_or_none()

    async def complete(self, run: Run, status: str) -> None:
        run.status = status
        # session tracks the change automatically; commit at request boundary

# routes/runs.py
from fastapi import APIRouter, Depends, HTTPException
from sqlalchemy.ext.asyncio import AsyncSession
from database import get_db
from repositories.run_repo import RunRepository
from uuid import UUID
import schemas   # Pydantic response models

router = APIRouter(prefix="/runs")

@router.post("/", response_model=schemas.RunOut, status_code=201)
async def create_run(body: schemas.RunCreate, db: AsyncSession = Depends(get_db)):
    repo = RunRepository(db)
    run = await repo.create(session_id=body.session_id)
    await db.commit()
    return run

@router.get("/{run_id}", response_model=schemas.RunDetail)
async def get_run(run_id: UUID, db: AsyncSession = Depends(get_db)):
    repo = RunRepository(db)
    run = await repo.get_with_events(run_id)
    if not run:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=404, detail="Run not found")
    return run
The following script runs standalone with SQLite — no PostgreSQL setup required. It creates tables, inserts a session and a run, then queries them back.

# Requires: pip install sqlalchemy aiosqlite
import asyncio
from sqlalchemy.ext.asyncio import create_async_engine, AsyncSession, async_sessionmaker
from sqlalchemy.orm import DeclarativeBase, Mapped, mapped_column, relationship
from sqlalchemy import String, ForeignKey, select
from uuid import UUID, uuid4

class Base(DeclarativeBase):
    pass

class AgentSession(Base):
    __tablename__ = "agent_sessions"
    id: Mapped[UUID] = mapped_column(primary_key=True, default=uuid4)
    user_id: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(String(64))
    runs: Mapped[list["Run"]] = relationship(back_populates="session")

class Run(Base):
    __tablename__ = "runs"
    id: Mapped[UUID] = mapped_column(primary_key=True, default=uuid4)
    session_id: Mapped[UUID] = mapped_column(ForeignKey("agent_sessions.id"))
    status: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(String(32), default="pending")
    session: Mapped[AgentSession] = relationship(back_populates="runs")

engine = create_async_engine("sqlite+aiosqlite:///:memory:", echo=True)
SessionLocal = async_sessionmaker(engine, class_=AsyncSession, expire_on_commit=False)

async def main():
    # Create schema
    async with engine.begin() as conn:
        await conn.run_sync(Base.metadata.create_all)

    async with SessionLocal() as db:
        # Insert
        sess = AgentSession(user_id="user-1")
        run = Run(session=sess, status="pending")
        db.add(sess)
        db.add(run)
        await db.commit()
        run_id = run.id

    async with SessionLocal() as db:
        # Query
        result = await db.execute(select(Run).where(Run.id == run_id))
        fetched = result.scalar_one()
        print(f"Run {fetched.id}: status={fetched.status}")

asyncio.run(main())
Example 2: eager loading and bulk event insert
A realistic pattern: load a run with all its tool events in one round-trip using
, then bulk-insert a batch of new events.

# Continuing from the models above
from sqlalchemy.orm import selectinload

class ToolEvent(Base):
    __tablename__ = "tool_events"
    id: Mapped[int] = mapped_column(primary_key=True, autoincrement=True)
    run_id: Mapped[UUID] = mapped_column(ForeignKey("runs.id"), index=True)
    tool_name: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(String(64))
    input_tokens: Mapped[int] = mapped_column(default=0)
    run: Mapped[Run] = relationship(back_populates="tool_events")

# Re-run Base.metadata.create_all after adding ToolEvent

async def load_run_with_events(db: AsyncSession, run_id: UUID) -> Run | None:
    stmt = (
        select(Run)
        .where(Run.id == run_id)
        .options(selectinload(Run.tool_events))   # issues 2 queries total
    )
    result = await db.execute(stmt)
    return result.scalar_one_or_none()

async def record_tool_calls(db: AsyncSession, run_id: UUID, calls: list[dict]):
    """Batch-insert tool events — one INSERT per flush, not per event."""
    events = [
        ToolEvent(run_id=run_id, tool_name=c["tool"], input_tokens=c["tokens"])
        for c in calls
    ]
    db.add_all(events)
    await db.flush()   # single INSERT with multiple value rows
    return events

A production agentic service might handle thousands of concurrent runs, each generating dozens of tool events. The following pattern is drawn from typical architectures at that scale: a FastAPI service backed by PostgreSQL, using async SQLAlchemy for all persistence operations, with Alembic managing schema evolution.

# Realistic RunService with cost tracking and status transitions
from sqlalchemy.ext.asyncio import AsyncSession
from sqlalchemy import select, func
from sqlalchemy.orm import selectinload
from uuid import UUID
from models import Run, ToolEvent

class RunService:
    def __init__(self, db: AsyncSession):
        self.db = db

    async def start_run(self, session_id: UUID) -> Run:
        run = Run(session_id=session_id, status="running")
        self.db.add(run)
        await self.db.flush()
        return run

    async def record_tool(self, run_id: UUID, tool_name: str,
                          input_tokens: int, output_tokens: int) -> ToolEvent:
        event = ToolEvent(
            run_id=run_id,
            tool_name=tool_name,
            input_tokens=input_tokens,
            output_tokens=output_tokens,
        )
        self.db.add(event)
        await self.db.flush()
        return event

    async def finish_run(self, run_id: UUID, success: bool) -> Run:
        result = await self.db.execute(select(Run).where(Run.id == run_id))
        run = result.scalar_one()
        run.status = "completed" if success else "failed"
        return run

    async def cost_summary(self, session_id: UUID) -> dict:
        """Total tokens across all runs in a session — one SQL aggregation."""
        stmt = (
            select(
                func.count(Run.id).label("run_count"),
                func.sum(ToolEvent.input_tokens).label("total_input"),
                func.sum(ToolEvent.output_tokens).label("total_output"),
            )
            .join(ToolEvent, ToolEvent.run_id == Run.id)
            .where(Run.session_id == session_id)
        )
        row = (await self.db.execute(stmt)).one()
        return {
            "runs": row.run_count,
            "input_tokens": row.total_input or 0,
            "output_tokens": row.total_output or 0,
        }
The key design decisions here: each agent invocation receives a new

via FastAPI dependency injection, so it never crosses transaction boundaries. The service owns the business rules (status transitions, cost aggregation); the routes just call service methods and commit. The cost_summary query drops to a SQL aggregation with
because looping over Python objects to sum tokens would be far less efficient at scale.
Using a sync session in an async route
The most common pitfall: creating a regular
) inside an async FastAPI route blocks the event loop thread while waiting for the database. Under load this serializes all concurrent requests.

# WRONG — accessing relationship after awaiting triggers MissingGreenlet
run = (await db.execute(select(Run).where(Run.id == run_id))).scalar_one()
events = run.tool_events   # ERROR: cannot do implicit I/O in async context

# CORRECT — eager load in the query
from sqlalchemy.orm import selectinload
stmt = select(Run).where(Run.id == run_id).options(selectinload(Run.tool_events))
run = (await db.execute(stmt)).scalar_one()
events = run.tool_events   # already loaded, no I/O needed

# WRONG — one commit (and one round-trip) per tool event
for event_data in tool_calls:
    event = ToolEvent(**event_data)
    db.add(event)
    await db.commit()   # 100 tool calls = 100 round-trips

# CORRECT — batch, then commit once
for event_data in tool_calls:
    db.add(ToolEvent(**event_data))
await db.commit()   # 1 round-trip regardless of event count
Storing LLM output blobs in ORM Text columns
Agent runs can produce large JSON traces — message histories, chain-of-thought logs — that may exceed tens of megabytes. Storing them in a

column bloats the table, slows pagination queries (Postgres reads all columns to compute row offsets), and makes backups expensive. Store the blob in object storage (S3, GCS) and keep only the key as a short string column in the ORM model.
in a context manager at the request boundary. Never share a session across requests or store it as a module-level global.
— decide at query time which relationships the request will need, and add the corresponding
option. Do not rely on implicit lazy loading in async code.
— wrap session calls behind a repository class. Routes stay thin; the repository becomes the seam where you inject a mock session in unit tests.
in production; use Alembic so schema changes are versioned, reviewable, and reversible.
to foreign keys, status columns, and any column used in filters. An unindexed status scan on a million-row runs table is a common performance incident.
on the engine or configure Python's logging to show SQLAlchemy output. Catching N+1 patterns early is far cheaper than diagnosing them under production load.
— the default expires all attributes after commit, forcing a re-SELECT when you access the object afterwards. Set
in the session factory to avoid this implicit round-trip.
Database connections are expensive to create. SQLAlchemy's built-in connection pool maintains a set of reusable connections. For async FastAPI, configure

(extra connections allowed under peak load). A common configuration for a moderate-traffic service: pool_size=10, max_overflow=20. This supports up to 30 concurrent database operations without connection exhaustion.

engine = create_async_engine(
    DATABASE_URL,
    pool_size=10,          # always-alive connections
    max_overflow=20,       # burst connections (returned to pool immediately)
    pool_timeout=30,       # seconds to wait for a connection before raising
    pool_recycle=1800,     # recycle connections older than 30 min (prevents stale)
)
followed by a single commit sends one SQL statement with multiple value tuples, which is significantly faster than individual adds and commits. For very high throughput (millions of rows), use
with the Core API, bypassing ORM object creation entirely.

from sqlalchemy.dialects.postgresql import insert

# Core bulk insert — fastest path, no ORM object overhead
async def bulk_insert_events(db: AsyncSession, events: list[dict]) -> None:
    await db.execute(
        insert(ToolEvent),
        events,   # list of dicts matching column names
    )
    await db.commit()
for pagination — never load full tables. Prefer keyset pagination (WHERE id > last_seen_id) over offset pagination for large datasets.
for aggregations in SQL rather than loading rows and computing in Python.
to all foreign keys and filter columns; verify the query plan with EXPLAIN ANALYZE in PostgreSQL.
Cache frequently-read, rarely-changed data (agent configurations, tool definitions) in an in-process dict or Redis rather than fetching from the ORM on every request.
— the foundation; provides the ORM, connection pool, Core query builder, and async support. Install:
— migration framework from the SQLAlchemy team; auto-generates migration scripts from model diffs. Install:
— Pydantic + SQLAlchemy hybrid by FastAPI's author. One class is both API schema and ORM model. Best for simpler domains; falls back to raw SQLAlchemy for complex queries. Install:
— the fastest async PostgreSQL driver for Python, required by SQLAlchemy's async engine for PostgreSQL.
— async SQLite driver; use for local development and tests without PostgreSQL.
— standard test stack; create a fresh in-memory database per test function for isolation.

# SQLModel example: one class for API and database
from sqlmodel import SQLModel, Field
from uuid import UUID, uuid4
from datetime import datetime
from sqlalchemy import func

class Run(SQLModel, table=True):
    id: UUID = Field(default_factory=uuid4, primary_key=True)
    session_id: UUID = Field(foreign_key="agentsession.id", index=True)
    status: str = Field(default="pending", max_length=32)
    # Pydantic validation + SQLAlchemy column in one declaration
The choice between an ORM, a query builder, or raw SQL affects how much of the database's power is available and how much Python code is needed to use it.
ORM-generated queries are always parameterized — user data is never interpolated into SQL strings. The risk surfaces only when developers bypass the ORM and use string formatting with

# INSECURE — SQL injection via f-string
stmt = text(f"SELECT * FROM runs WHERE user_id = '{user_input}'")

# SECURE — bind parameter, even with text()
stmt = text("SELECT * FROM runs WHERE user_id = :uid").bindparams(uid=user_input)
result = await db.execute(stmt)
Always use the session as a context manager. If the route handler raises an exception before committing, the context manager rolls back the transaction automatically, preventing partial writes.

async def get_db():
    async with AsyncSessionLocal() as session:
        try:
            yield session
            await session.commit()
        except Exception:
            await session.rollback()
            raise
on the engine so SQLAlchemy tests each connection before issuing a query. This automatically recovers from database restarts or network interruptions that invalidate idle connections.
Never hardcode the database URL. Use environment variables or a secrets manager (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault). Pass the URL to
at startup from an environment variable. Rotate credentials without code changes by updating the secret and restarting the application.
ORMs map Python classes to database tables and method calls to parameterized SQL, eliminating boilerplate and SQL injection risk for standard CRUD operations.
integrates cleanly with FastAPI; always use
to avoid implicit re-fetches after commit.
Never access a lazy-loaded relationship in async code — the result is a
error. Declare all required relationships with
The repository pattern keeps session calls out of route handlers, makes the data layer independently testable, and gives a clean seam for injecting mock sessions in unit tests.
Alembic is the standard migration tool: auto-generate scripts from model diffs, commit them to version control, and apply them with alembic upgrade head rather than recreating tables in production.
Drop to raw SQL or SQLAlchemy Core for heavy analytics, bulk inserts, and database-specific features that the ORM query builder cannot express cleanly.
Store LLM output blobs (message history, traces) in object storage and keep only the reference key in the ORM model — oversized Text columns degrade query and backup performance.
Set pool_pre_ping=True for connection reliability and configure pool_size and max_overflow to match the expected concurrency of the agentic service.
